question.label: The real version
question.name: Tell me about your week — the real version.
question.description: Kim Scott's 'care personally' made concrete. Signals that a performed answer won't cut it.

answer: Yeah she saiid it was nice to complete the course,
note: Pivot/off-topic answer gives no clear signal on wellbeing or engagement; likely references someone else's reaction to completing the course.

question.label: Self-read opener
question.name: Before I share my view, how do you think the last stretch has gone?
question.description: Anchors the conversation in their self-assessment first; gap vs manager's view becomes a signal.

answer: i want to now how i can do the marking easier, it took sl long
note: Pivoted from self-assessment to a specific friction point — marking feels inefficient and time-heavy, signaling low clarity on how to do the work effectively and mild strain on engagement.

question.label: Marking friction
question.name: Marking took so long — what specifically made it slow?
question.description: Drills into the concrete marking friction she raised so the conversation stays anchored in her actual work.

answer: there was a marked improvement in the sutdents
note: Pivot/off-topic: she answered with learner improvement rather than what made marking slow; this also volunteers class impact evidence worth using next.

question.label: Class impact evidence
question.name: What changed for learners because of your work on the new university class?
question.description: Surfaces concrete outcomes and leverage from her work in the class.

note: [SHALLOW] 'They are more confident' names a vague outcome with no concrete evidence of what changed.

question.label: Confidence evidence
question.name: You said 'more confident' — what did they do differently that showed that?
question.description: Clarifies the vague outcome by asking for observable learner behaviour as evidence.

answer: they didnt wait to be asked to talk , theyu just go up and went for it
note: She gave concrete behavioural evidence of learner confidence and initiative, indicating strong learner growth and a mild positive engagement signal.

question.label: Student initiative
question.name: Those moments where they 'just went for it' — what did you do that caused that?
question.description: Pins the observed learner behaviour back to her specific coaching actions before moving on.

answer: I never said it was taking more effort
note: [SHALLOW] 'I never said it was taking more effort' does not answer what she did to cause the student initiative; [BUDGET-STARVED]

question.label: Hidden friction
question.name: Where is the coaching work still taking more effort than it should?
question.description: Finds the specific friction points that are easy to miss when the overall picture looks strong.

note: [SHALLOW] 'i never said that' disputes the premise but does not name where effort is high or low; [THREAD-DEFERRED-WINDDOWN] denied premise of hidden friction, but wind-down requires advancing to cause/commit.

question.label: Input quality
question.name: What feedback are you not getting enough of right now?
question.description: Surfaces gaps in the feedback loop from learners, peers, or leadership.

answer: they loved the class and sad it was over
note: Pivot/off-topic answer praised learner reaction rather than naming missing feedback; final turn so queue advances to the closer.

question.label: Close the loop
question.name: Given what we talked through, what is the first concrete thing you want to change before we meet again?
question.description: Ends on a specific commitment with a near-term action.

answer: i want to have a way to do less marking
note: She names a concrete improvement target — reducing marking load — which shows some next-step clarity and a modest change orientation.
